title: Thinking Like a Programmer Without Fear

learning_objectives: Build logical thinking for technical problem-solving.

blocks.title: Week 3 Focus
blocks.content: Programming is introduced as giving precise instructions.

items.label: Sequence
items.description: Understand ordered steps in a process.

items.label: Variable
items.description: A named place to store a value or piece of information.

items.label: Condition
items.description: A check that decides what happens next.

items.label: Loop
items.description: Repeating steps until a rule changes.

blocks.title: Key Message
blocks.content: The goal is not to turn students into programmers in one week. The goal is to make logic feel familiar.

activity.title: Fix the Broken Logic
activity.instructions: Arrange the steps into a correct logical order.
activity.success_criteria: Create a flow with no missing step.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of a flowchart?
options: To show the steps and decisions in a process
questions.explanation: Flowcharts visualize steps and decisions clearly.

questions.prompt: Which idea is closest to a condition in logic?
options: If this is true, do the next step
Store a file in a folder
questions.explanation: A condition checks whether something is true before an action happens.

options: Step-by-step logic written in simple language
questions.explanation: Pseudocode helps people describe logic without needing strict programming syntax.

reflection.prompt: Write a short set of instructions for a routine task in your own field. Then name one place where unclear logic could cause confusion.
reflection.placeholder: Task: registering a student. Confusion can happen if we do not clearly say when to check missing documents.

teacher_notes: Use an everyday process like making tea or sending an email as the first logic exercise.
Ask students to spot missing steps rather than writing perfect logic immediately.
